mappings.rationale: Core VSI for endocrine systems (C), covering vertebrate hormonal coordination, pituitary gland, hypothalamus control, and the relationship between endocrine and neural regulation.
Core VSI for nervous systems (D), covering the brain and its components, cerebral cortex, basal ganglia, spinal cord, and neural information processing.
Directly covers sensory reception (B), specifically photoreceptors, retinal processing, optic nerve transmission, and evolution of visual sensory systems.
Core VSI for the biological clock (E), covering solar-day rhythms, the molecular clock mechanism, metabolism, and factors affecting biological periodicities.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Steven W. Lockley, Russell G. Foster
mappings.rationale: Covers dormancy and metabolic rate changes (A.6), brain-wave rhythms (E.1), and melatonin's role in biological periodicity (E).
Addresses homeostasis (A), physiological regulation mechanisms, and the interplay of endocrine and nervous systems in maintaining steady states.
Covers the nervous system's role in information processing and integration (D.2), including brain imaging and cognition in the vertebrate brain (D.4).
Addresses sensory information processing (B.3), interactions between sense cells and sensory neurons, and the physiology of the visual system.
Covers chemoreceptors (B.1.b) and olfactory sensory systems, illustrating chemoreception as a fundamental class of sensory reception.
Covers invertebrate nervous systems (D.3) including diffuse and centralized systems across Annelida, Cnidaria, and other phyla.
Relevant to invertebrate endocrine systems (C.3) in insects and invertebrate nervous system organization.
Covers nerve impulse transmission, action potentials, and the sympathetic nervous system (D.2, D.4.b), illustrating neural regulation mechanisms.
Addresses higher-level integration of sensory information in the brain and the evolution of consciousness as nervous system complexity (D.4.e).
Covers nociception as a sensory modality (B.1.b mechanoreceptors) and neural processing pathways for pain signals.
